title: An open letter from the village librarian
summary: When the district council writes that nobody reads books anymore, the librarian replies — politely, firmly, and with a list of who actually walks through the door.
body:
To the members of the District Council,

You have written that you are considering closing our library because, in your words, 'people don't read books like they used to.' I will not argue with the statistic. I keep the borrowing records myself. The number of books leaving this building has fallen for ten years in a row.

But I would like, before you decide, to tell you what a library is in 2026 — not what it was in 1986. I think we have been describing different things, and the word 'library' has been doing the confusing.

In a normal week, here is what passes through this building. A four-year-old boy comes for Tuesday story hour because his mother works two jobs, and those thirty minutes are the only time an adult reads aloud to him. A schoolgirl whose family of six lives in two rooms studies for her exams here; she has told me, without drama, that the library is the only quiet she gets. An old man, recently widowed, comes every day to read the newspaper. He does not always finish it. He needs a chair, near other chairs, where no one asks how he is.

A young woman from a village three kilometres away learns to use a computer here on Wednesdays — for an application form that the council itself now requires online. A grandmother who can read but cannot write English brings her grandson's school letters to me, and we translate them together. A retired teacher runs a free Vietnamese-French exchange in the back room on Saturdays. None of these activities involve books in the way the 1986 brochure imagined.

I am not asking you to fund a building full of books that nobody borrows. I am asking you to keep funding a building that is — quietly, daily — a warm place, a quiet place, a free place, and a place where someone (it happens to be me) knows everyone's name.

If you close us, the books will be moved or sold. That part is straightforward. The harder part is the rest — the chair near other chairs, the half-finished newspaper, the thirty minutes of being read to. Those don't move. They stop.

If, after all this, you still feel the library should close, then please rename it before you do. Calling us a 'library' makes it easy to argue that, in 2026, libraries are unnecessary. Call us a 'shared room of the village' instead. Then read what you wrote, and ask yourself again.

Yours, with great respect,
Mrs Hue Thi Tran
Librarian, since 1993

annotations.meaningVi: dịch (từ ngôn ngữ này sang ngôn ngữ khác)
tờ rơi giới thiệu, tập sách mỏng

grammarNotes.title: Polite formal phrases: 'I would like…' / 'I am not asking… I am asking…'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong văn phong trang trọng (thư chính thức), người viết thường dùng 'I would like to…' (Tôi muốn…) thay vì 'I want to…' để giảm cảm giác đòi hỏi. Mẫu 'I am not asking you to X. I am asking you to Y.' là cách lịch sự nhưng kiên quyết để làm rõ ý đúng và sửa một hiểu lầm. Trong bài: 'I am not asking you to fund a building full of books that nobody borrows. I am asking you to keep funding a building that is…'
grammarNotes.bodyEn: In formal writing such as official letters, 'I would like to…' is politer than 'I want to…' — it softens the request. The pattern 'I am not asking you to X. I am asking you to Y.' is firm but polite, used to correct a misunderstanding. From the letter: 'I am not asking you to fund a building full of books that nobody borrows. I am asking you to keep funding a building that is…'

To the members of the District Council,

You have written that you are considering {{b1}} our library because, in your words, 'people don't read books like they used to.' I will not argue with the {{b2}}. I keep the {{b3}} records myself. The number of books leaving this building has {{b4}} for ten years in a row.

But I would like, before you decide, to tell you what a library is in 2026 — not what it was in {{b5}}. I think we have been describing different things, and the word 'library' has been doing the confusing.

In a normal week, here is what passes through this building. A four-year-old boy comes for Tuesday {{b6}} hour because his mother works two jobs, and those thirty minutes are the only time an adult reads aloud to him. A schoolgirl whose family of six lives in two rooms studies for her exams here; she has told me, without drama, that the library is the only {{b7}} she gets. An old man, recently {{b8}}, comes every day to read the newspaper. He does not always finish it. He needs a chair, near other chairs, where no one asks how he is.

A young woman from a village three kilometres away learns to use a {{b9}} here on Wednesdays — for an application form that the council itself now requires online. A grandmother who can read but cannot write English brings her grandson's school letters to me, and we {{b10}} them together. A retired teacher runs a free Vietnamese-French exchange in the back room on Saturdays. None of these activities involve books in the way the 1986 brochure imagined.

I am not asking you to fund a building full of books that nobody borrows. I am asking you to keep funding a building that is — quietly, daily — a {{b11}} place, a quiet place, a free place, and a place where someone (it happens to be me) knows everyone's name.

If you close us, the books will be moved or sold. That part is straightforward. The harder part is the rest — the chair near other chairs, the half-finished newspaper, the thirty minutes of being read to. Those don't move. They stop.

If, after all this, you still feel the library should close, then please {{b12}} it before you do. Calling us a 'library' makes it easy to argue that, in 2026, libraries are unnecessary. Call us a 'shared room of the village' instead. Then read what you wrote, and ask yourself again.

Yours, with great respect,
Mrs Hue Thi Tran
Librarian, since 1993

blanks.explanation: The council is 'considering closing our library' — the conflict the whole letter responds to.
'I will not argue with the statistic' — she accepts the number, then re-frames what it means.
Borrowing records — the log of books taken out.
Borrowing has 'fallen for ten years in a row' — declined steadily.
She contrasts 2026 with 1986 — what a library meant forty years ago versus now.
Tuesday story hour — when an adult reads aloud to children.
'The library is the only quiet she gets.' Her family of six lives in two rooms.
An old man, recently widowed (whose wife has just died), needs the company of other chairs.
She learns to use a computer — for an application the council itself requires online.
The grandmother brings school letters and 'we translate them together'.
A warm, quiet, free place — the librarian's three short words for what the building actually is.
'Please rename it before you do' — if you must close it, change the word first, because the word is part of the argument.

translationVi:
Kính gửi các thành viên Hội đồng Huyện,

Quý vị đã viết rằng quý vị đang cân nhắc đóng cửa thư viện của chúng tôi vì, theo lời quý vị, 'người ta không đọc sách như xưa nữa.' Tôi sẽ không tranh cãi với con số đó. Chính tôi giữ sổ ghi mượn. Số lượng sách rời khỏi tòa nhà này đã giảm mười năm liền.

Nhưng trước khi quý vị quyết định, tôi muốn nói cho quý vị biết một thư viện trong năm 2026 là gì — không phải nó từng là gì vào năm 1986. Tôi nghĩ chúng ta đã và đang mô tả những thứ khác nhau, và chính từ 'thư viện' đang gây ra phần lớn sự nhầm lẫn.

Trong một tuần bình thường, đây là những gì đi qua tòa nhà này. Một cậu bé bốn tuổi đến giờ kể chuyện thứ Ba vì mẹ cậu làm hai công việc, và ba mươi phút đó là khoảng thời gian duy nhất có một người lớn đọc to cho cậu nghe. Một nữ sinh mà gia đình sáu người sống trong hai gian phòng đến đây học cho kỳ thi; cô đã nói với tôi, không hề kịch tính, rằng thư viện là sự yên tĩnh duy nhất cô có. Một ông cụ vừa góa vợ đến mỗi ngày để đọc báo. Cụ không phải lúc nào cũng đọc hết. Cụ chỉ cần một cái ghế, gần những cái ghế khác, nơi không ai hỏi cụ rằng cụ có khỏe không.

Một phụ nữ trẻ từ một xã cách đây ba cây số học sử dụng máy tính ở đây vào thứ Tư — để điền một mẫu đơn xin mà chính ủy ban giờ đây yêu cầu làm trực tuyến. Một bà nội biết đọc nhưng không biết viết tiếng Anh đem các bức thư từ trường của cháu đến chỗ tôi, và chúng tôi dịch cùng nhau. Một thầy giáo đã nghỉ hưu mở một buổi trao đổi tiếng Việt-Pháp miễn phí trong căn phòng phía sau vào sáng thứ Bảy. Không hoạt động nào trong số này liên quan đến sách theo cách mà tờ rơi giới thiệu năm 1986 đã hình dung.

Tôi không yêu cầu quý vị tài trợ cho một tòa nhà đầy sách mà không ai mượn. Tôi đang yêu cầu quý vị tiếp tục tài trợ cho một tòa nhà mà — một cách lặng lẽ, hằng ngày — là một nơi ấm áp, một nơi yên tĩnh, một nơi miễn phí, và một nơi có một người (tình cờ là tôi) biết tên tất cả mọi người.

Nếu quý vị đóng cửa chúng tôi, sách sẽ được chuyển đi hoặc bán. Phần đó đơn giản. Phần khó hơn là phần còn lại — cái ghế gần những cái ghế khác, tờ báo đọc dở, ba mươi phút được nghe đọc. Những thứ đó không di chuyển. Chúng dừng lại.

Nếu, sau tất cả những điều này, quý vị vẫn cảm thấy thư viện nên đóng cửa, thì xin hãy đổi tên nó trước khi làm vậy. Gọi chúng tôi là 'thư viện' khiến cho dễ lập luận rằng, vào năm 2026, các thư viện không còn cần thiết. Hãy gọi chúng tôi là 'gian phòng chung của làng' thay vì thế. Rồi đọc lại những gì quý vị đã viết, và tự hỏi mình lần nữa.

Kính thư,
Trần Thị Huệ
Thủ thư, từ năm 1993

questions.prompt: Who has written this letter, and to whom?
options: A district council member to villagers
The village librarian to the district council
questions.explanation: The salutation is 'To the members of the District Council' and the sign-off is from 'Mrs Hue Thi Tran, Librarian, since 1993'.
questions.hint: Look at the opening and the closing of the letter.

questions.prompt: Why is the council considering closing the library?
options: Because the librarian wants to retire
Because, in the council's words, 'people don't read books like they used to'
Because the rent is too high
questions.explanation: 'You are considering closing our library because, in your words, "people don't read books like they used to."'

questions.prompt: What does the librarian say she will NOT argue about?
options: The statistic that borrowing has fallen for ten years
questions.explanation: 'I will not argue with the statistic. I keep the borrowing records myself.'
questions.hint: Look at the end of the first paragraph.

questions.prompt: Who comes for the Tuesday story hour, and why is it important?
options: A four-year-old boy — those thirty minutes are the only time an adult reads aloud to him
An old man, to read the newspaper
questions.explanation: 'A four-year-old boy comes for Tuesday story hour because his mother works two jobs…'
questions.hint: Look at the first example in paragraph 3.

questions.prompt: Why does the recently widowed man come every day?
options: To read the newspaper — but mainly to sit near other chairs where no one asks how he is
To learn to use a computer
questions.explanation: 'He needs a chair, near other chairs, where no one asks how he is.'
questions.hint: Look at the third example in paragraph 3.

questions.prompt: Why does the young woman come on Wednesdays?
options: To learn to use a computer — for an application the council itself requires online
questions.explanation: 'For an application form that the council itself now requires online.'
questions.hint: Look at paragraph 4. The irony is important.

questions.prompt: What is the librarian actually asking the council to fund?
options: Not books — a warm, quiet, free place where someone knows everyone's name
questions.explanation: 'I am not asking you to fund a building full of books… I am asking you to keep funding a building that is — quietly, daily — a warm place, a quiet place, a free place…'

questions.prompt: According to the librarian, what will happen to the books if the library closes — and what will happen to 'the rest'?
options: The books will be moved or sold; the chair, the newspaper, the story hour will simply stop
The books will be burned and the rest moved
questions.explanation: 'If you close us, the books will be moved or sold… The harder part is the rest… Those don't move. They stop.'

questions.prompt: What does the librarian suggest the council do if they really decide to close the library?
options: Open another library in a bigger building
Rename it before closing — calling it a 'library' makes it too easy to argue libraries are unnecessary
questions.explanation: 'Please rename it before you do. Calling us a "library" makes it easy to argue that… libraries are unnecessary. Call us a "shared room of the village" instead.'
questions.hint: Look at the last full paragraph before the sign-off.

questions.prompt: What is the tone of the letter?
options: Polite but firm — she accepts the data, redefines the term, and re-frames the decision
questions.explanation: She does not argue with the council's numbers, but she argues — politely — with the council's understanding of the word 'library'.
questions.hint: Notice how she begins by agreeing with the council, and then turns the question.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The librarian argues that the word 'library' itself makes it easier to close the building, because the word makes the building sound unnecessary. What other places in your community might be in the same situation — where the old word for them no longer captures what they actually do for people? What would be a better name?
